patient.name: Emily Chen
patient.chiefComplaint: Severe asthma exacerbation unresponsive to rescue inhaler

history.hpi: 19-year-old female college student found in her dorm room by her roommate, tripoding on the edge of the bed in severe respiratory distress. Over an hour of repeated rescue inhaler use with no relief. Has had worsening symptoms for three days after a viral URI. Two prior hospitalizations for asthma this year including one ICU admission with intubation six months ago.
pastMedical: Prior ICU admission with intubation (6 months ago)
history.lastOralIntake: Water; no solid food in 24 hours due to dyspnea
history.events: Started with mild wheeze three days ago. Symptoms progressed over the evening. Used rescue inhaler repeatedly for the past hour with no improvement. Roommate called 911 when she could no longer speak.

scene.dispatch: Medic 5, respond code 3 to Westfield Residence Hall room 308, for a 19-year-old female with severe asthma, difficulty breathing.
scene.location: College dormitory room, third floor, accessed via hallway and elevator. Narrow room with bunk beds. Patient on the edge of the bottom bunk, tripoding.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Keep the roommate present to help keep the patient calm.
scene.bystanders: Roommate is present, has the patient's medication list on her phone, and confirms the prior ICU admission/intubation history
visualCues: Young adult female tripoding on the edge of the bunk, leaning forward with both hands on her knees
Visibly exhausted, pale with perioral cyanosis
Accessory muscle use prominent — strap muscles and intercostals
Empty albuterol HFA on the nightstand
Unable to complete a sentence — single words only
Minimal air movement audible on breathing; no wheezing heard from across the room (silent chest)

phases.name: Arrival — Silent Chest, Exhausted Patient
phases.description: Near-fatal asthma presentation. Silent chest bilaterally is NOT reassurance — it signals critical airflow obstruction with no air moving. Prior ICU/intubation history and recent rescue inhaler abuse are risk factors for death. Patient is fatiguing and may tire out to respiratory arrest if not aggressively treated.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent; no upper airway obstruction; speaking single words only
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Rapid shallow respirations at 36/min; prominent accessory muscle use; intercostal retractions; silent chest bilaterally on auscultation (NO wheezing heard)
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Tachycardic with pulsus paradoxus palpable; warm peripheries
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Purposeful but exhausted; gripping the edge of the bed
otherFindings: Tripod position, refuses to lie back
Prior near-fatal asthma (ICU/intubation) is the strongest predictor of death in this episode
Silent chest = severe airflow obstruction, not improvement

expectedActions.action: Allow patient to remain in position of comfort (tripod, upright)
Apply oxygen with combination Albuterol 5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized via mask; repeat albuterol at unlimited frequency for ongoing distress
Establish IV access
Administer Methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg IV (120 mg for 60 kg patient, max 125 mg) — patient is critically ill, use IV route
Administer Magnesium sulfate 40 mg/kg IV (2400 mg, capped at max 2 g) in 100 mL NS infused over 10-15 minutes; watch for hypotension
expectedActions.rationale: Magnesium is specifically indicated for severe bronchoconstriction with impending respiratory failure. A silent chest patient with fatigue qualifies.

expectedActions.action: Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography (expect shark-fin waveform)
Prepare to apply CPAP (starting 5 cmH2O PEEP) with in-line nebulizer if patient tolerates the mask and remains conscious
Actively coach the patient through the CPAP mask application to improve tolerance — anxiety here is physiologic, not psychological

phases.name: Default Path — Responding to Treatment
phases.description: After 10-15 minutes of aggressive treatment (bronchodilators, steroid, magnesium, CPAP), the patient starts to improve. Air movement returns — now audibly wheezing (an IMPROVEMENT from silent chest). SpO2 climbing, RR decreasing, speaking short phrases. Continue therapy and transport.
phases.triggerCondition: Clinical improvement after magnesium, nebulizers, and CPAP

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent; now audibly wheezing (improvement — air moving)
clinicalPresentation.breathing: CPAP in progress with in-line albuterol; accessory muscle use decreasing; expiratory phase prolonged
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Less rigid, able to relax shoulders
otherFindings: Waveform capnography still shows shark-fin shape but less pronounced
EtCO2 trending down as minute ventilation improves

expectedActions.action: Continue back-to-back Albuterol 5 mg nebulized via CPAP circuit
DO NOT discontinue CPAP until arrival at ED bedside with replacement ready
expectedActions.rationale: Breaking the mask seal causes atelectasis and sudden decompensation.

expectedActions.action: Transport to ED with early notification that this is a near-fatal asthma patient
Continuous reassessment — watch for re-deterioration and fatigue
NS fluid bolus 500 mL if BP trends down from magnesium or beta-agonist-related vasodilation

phases.name: Branch: Tiring Out — Peri-Arrest Requiring RSI
phases.description: Patient fatigues before bronchodilators and magnesium take effect. Mental status deteriorates, CPAP is no longer tolerated, and respiratory arrest is imminent. Crew must transition to BVM and then RSI while the bronchospasm is still present. This is the worst airway in EMS: high airway pressures, air trapping, high pneumothorax risk.
phases.triggerCondition: Deteriorating mental status, SpO2 falling, or loss of respiratory drive during treatment

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent but no longer cooperating with CPAP
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Slowing respiratory rate (ominous) with agonal pattern; silent chest again
otherFindings: Decreasing respiratory rate in a severe asthmatic is a pre-arrest sign — don't be reassured
CPAP mask pulled off by fatiguing patient

expectedActions.action: Administer Epinephrine 0.3 mg IM (1 mg/mL) in the lateral thigh — rescue therapy for impending respiratory failure despite max nebulizer and IV therapy
Begin 2-person BVM with PEEP 5 cmH2O — ventilate SLOWLY (6-8 breaths/min) with long exhalation to prevent breath stacking and pneumothorax
expectedActions.rationale: Asthmatic airway physiology: the danger is air trapping. Slow rate + long exhalation (I:E ratio 1:4 or 1:5) is mandatory.

expectedActions.action: Preoxygenate as best as possible recognizing severe V/Q mismatch
Administer Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV push (90 mg for 60 kg patient) as induction agent — ketamine has bronchodilator properties and is the preferred induction agent for asthma intubation
expectedActions.rationale: Ketamine is the induction agent of choice in status asthmaticus for its bronchodilator properties.

expectedActions.action: Administer Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV push (60 mg for 60 kg patient)
Perform video laryngoscopy intubation; 7.5 mm ETT
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography

phases.name: Post-Intubation Ventilation Strategy
phases.description: Tube confirmed. The hard part is ventilating — high airway pressures, severe air trapping, and risk of pneumothorax. Low rate, long exhalation, small tidal volumes, and permissive hypercapnia are the rules. If the patient decompensates suddenly after intubation, disconnect the circuit and let them exhale.

clinicalPresentation.airway: 7.5 mm ETT at 21 cm, cuff inflated, secured
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Manually ventilated at 6-8 breaths/min with prolonged expiratory phase
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Tachycardic, BP low-normal (watch for air-trapping-induced hypotension)
otherFindings: EtCO2 still elevated — permissive hypercapnia accepted to avoid barotrauma
Monitor for sudden decompensation suggesting pneumothorax

expectedActions.action: Ventilate at 6-8 breaths/min with long exhalation (I:E 1:4-1:5); accept permissive hypercapnia (EtCO2 up to 60-70 acceptable in asthma)
expectedActions.rationale: Standard ventilation rates cause breath stacking and pneumothorax in severe asthma.

expectedActions.action: Low tidal volumes 6 mL/kg IBW
Post-intubation sedation with Ketamine 0.5-1 mg/kg IV maintenance infusion or boluses (30-60 mg) + Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg IV
Continue in-line albuterol nebulizer through the ETT circuit
If sudden hypotension/desaturation post-intubation: disconnect circuit to allow exhalation; assess for pneumothorax (DOPE: displacement, obstruction, pneumothorax, equipment)
expectedActions.rationale: Breath stacking and pneumothorax are the common post-intubation killers in asthma.

expectedActions.action: Emergent transport to ED with early notification

criticalActions: Recognize silent chest as a SEVERE finding, not improvement
Recognize prior intubation history as the strongest risk factor for death
Administer combination Albuterol 5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized with unlimited repetition
Administer IV Methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg (max 125 mg) OR Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg (max 16 mg)
Administer Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 10-15 minutes
Apply CPAP with in-line nebulizer and coach the patient to tolerate it
IM Epinephrine 0.3 mg for impending respiratory failure despite optimal nebulizer therapy
If respiratory failure: RSI with Ketamine + Rocuronium
Post-intubation: SLOW ventilation (6-8/min) with long exhalation to prevent air trapping
expectedActions: IV access for magnesium and rescue medications
bonusActions: Recognize shark-fin capnography as obstructive physiology
Articulate DOPE mnemonic for post-intubation deterioration
Consider disconnecting circuit for apneic exhalation if breath stacking is suspected

learningObjectives: Recognize near-fatal asthma and apply MATC Respiratory Distress protocol aggressively
Use CPAP with in-line bronchodilators to avoid intubation
Understand magnesium sulfate as a rescue therapy in severe bronchoconstriction
Apply a modified ventilation strategy to avoid breath stacking in asthma
Use ketamine as the induction agent of choice in status asthmaticus
discussionQuestions: Why is a silent chest more concerning than loud wheezing?
How does magnesium sulfate work in severe asthma?
When do you give IM epinephrine in asthma, and how is it different from the anaphylaxis dose?
What is 'breath stacking' and how do you prevent it?
What does the DOPE mnemonic stand for and when do you use it?
commonPitfalls: Being falsely reassured by a silent chest or a decreasing respiratory rate
Ventilating intubated asthmatics at 'normal' rates and causing pneumothorax
Removing CPAP in the ambulance when the patient improves
Under-dosing magnesium or omitting it entirely
Choosing etomidate over ketamine for induction when bronchodilator effect would help
keyTakeaways: CPAP + aggressive medical therapy is the goal; intubation is a last resort
Ventilation strategy in intubated asthma: slow, low volume, long exhalation, tolerate hypercapnia
Prior intubation is the best predictor of death — take the history seriously
